extraction_metadata.source_document: Environmental-Economic Accounting Transitions in the Western Indian Ocean: A Comprehensive Analysis of SEEA Ecosystem Accounts and Publications in Nairobi Convention Member States
extraction_metadata.notes: Initial extraction + 3 additional sources from Academic PDF folder (Uganda, Rwanda, KwaZulu-Natal)

sources.title: System of Environmental-Economic Accounting—Ecosystem Accounting (SEEA EA)
sources.abstract: International statistical standard for Environmental-Economic Accounting with ecosystem focus. Framework for organizing data on habitats and landscapes, measuring ecosystem services, tracking ecosystem asset changes, and linking environmental data to economic activities. Adopted as official statistical standard by UNSC in March 2021.

metadata.notes: Core framework document; mandatory reference for all SEEA EA work

sources.title: Land and Terrestrial Ecosystem Accounts, 1990 to 2014
sources.abstract: First systematic national-level SEEA EA ecosystem extent and condition accounts for South Africa. Documents land cover changes 1990-2014 across 120 years of habitat transitions and shifts in ecosystem type distributions. Provides benchmark for African implementation of ecosystem accounting.

metadata.notes: Foundational African SEEA EA implementation; directly applicable to Madagascar ecosystem accounting

sources.title: Natural Capital 2: Accounts for Protected Areas, 1900–2020
sources.abstract: Standardized national accounts summarizing 120 years of changes in protected area network composition at national, provincial, and biome levels. Innovates thematic accounting for conservation purposes.
sources.methodology: historical PA network analysis + spatial accounting

metadata.notes: Thematic accounting innovation; directly relevant to Madagascar PA condition analysis

sources.title: Potential Costs and Benefits of Addressing Land Degradation in the Thukela Catchment: SEEA EA Ecosystem Services Scenario Analysis
sources.abstract: Experimental ecosystem services accounts in physical and monetary terms for 11 services (2005-2011) applied to policy scenario analysis. Demonstrates restoration cost-benefit analysis: halting degradation yields positive net economic benefits; restoration more cost-effective than status quo.

metadata.notes: Demonstrates ecosystem services account application to policy questions; relevant for Madagascar services valuation

sources.title: National Plan for the Advancement of Environmental-Economic Accounting (NP-AEEA)
sources.abstract: Phased strategy for Kenya's transition from SEEA Central Framework to full SEEA EA implementation. Aligns statistical efforts with Africa Agenda 2063 and Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. Foundational document for East African SEEA EA adoption.

metadata.notes: Key reference for regional SEEA EA implementation strategy

sources.title: Kenya: Physical Energy Supply and Use Tables 2018
sources.abstract: First set of pilot SEEA Central Framework tables for Kenya, demonstrating integration of energy data with economic statistics. Foundation for subsequent ecosystem accounting work.

metadata.notes: First SEEA integration work in Kenya; foundation for later EA transition

sources.title: Ecosystem Services and Assets for Kenya's Coastal Regions: SEEA EA Coastal Assessment
sources.abstract: UNEP-funded study quantifying and valuing ecosystem services and assets for Kenya's coastal regions following SEEA EA methodology. Involves complex remote sensing and GIS modeling for fisheries and marine-based services assessment.

metadata.notes: Key reference for coastal service valuation methods; directly applicable to Madagascar marine services

sources.title: Madagascar: Physical Stock Accounts for Forests
authors: Wealth Accounting and the Valuation of Ecosystem Services (WAVES) Program
sources.abstract: Satellite imagery-based ecosystem extent accounts for Madagascar's forests (2005-2013). Demonstrates dense humid forest area dominance with significant losses (25% in sparse dry forest areas). Foundation for Madagascar's natural capital accounting.

metadata.notes: Pioneering Madagascar SEEA EA work; demonstrates methodological approach for national accounts

sources.title: Madagascar: Physical Stock and Flow Accounts for Water Resources
authors: Wealth Accounting and the Valuation of Ecosystem Services (WAVES) Program
sources.abstract: SEEA EA water resource accounts for Madagascar, demonstrating flow and stock assessment methodologies for freshwater systems and their ecosystem services.

metadata.notes: Part of Madagascar's foundational WAVES accounts; water-marine ecosystem linkages

sources.title: Ecosystem Natural Capital Accounting for the Mahavavy-Kinkony Complex, Madagascar: 2013-2018 Assessment
sources.abstract: High-resolution ecosystem natural capital accounting (ENCA) for protected area complex in Madagascar using 15-30m satellite data. Findings: 25% loss in sparse dry forest (charcoal production), 60% increase in rice cultivation with aquatic silting impacts (2013-2018).

metadata.notes: Recent Madagascar-specific ecosystem accounting; demonstrates land use pressures on protected areas

sources.title: Experimental Ecosystem Natural Capital Accounts - Mauritius Case Study
sources.abstract: Pilot application of SEEA EA methodology to Mauritius (2000-2010 data). Tests feasibility and validates ecosystem accounting framework for SIDS context. Foundation for Mauritius ecosystem accounting implementation.

metadata.notes: Key SIDS example; demonstrates ecosystem accounting adaptation for island economies

sources.title: Improved Ecosystem Condition but Reduced Services? Modelling Corporate Land Use Effects on a Watershed in Mauritius
sources.abstract: SEEA EA application to evaluate corporate land use development scenarios in Mauritius. Finds that financially maximizing urban scenario improves biophysical condition but halves ecosystem service values vs. baseline. Critical finding: biophysical condition ≠ service provision.

metadata.notes: Recent finding showing critical distinction between condition and services; highly relevant to Madagascar valuation work

sources.title: Mozambique National Energy Account: Pilot Study
sources.abstract: Pilot SEEA Central Framework energy accounts for Mozambique in physical and monetary terms. Foundation for subsequent ecosystem accounting work. Demonstrates energy-economy integration for resource planning.

metadata.notes: Foundational work for Mozambique; part of regional SEEA capacity building

sources.title: Artisanal Fisheries and Climate Change in Mozambique
sources.abstract: Assessment of coastal population (66% dependent on fisheries; 2-4% of GDP contribution) and climate change impacts on artisanal fisheries. Baseline data for future ecosystem service and condition accounts. Not yet formally integrated into SEEA EA framework.

metadata.notes: Important baseline for Mozambique coastal fisheries; sets stage for SEEA EA integration

sources.title: Ocean Accounts: A Comprehensive Framework for Blue Economy Accounting
sources.abstract: Technical guidance for ocean accounting integrating environmental, social, and economic information for marine ecosystem management. Covers blue carbon, coastal protection, fisheries services. Essential for SIDS and coastal nations. Supports Blue Financing and impact investing.

metadata.notes: Specialized framework for marine/ocean accounts; highly relevant to Madagascar marine ecosystem accounting

sources.title: Northern Mozambique Channel (NoCaMo) Project: Natural Capital Assessment
sources.abstract: Regional assessment of marine and coastal natural capital in Northern Mozambique Channel. Findings: US $5.7 billion annual ecosystem value, but only US $615 million captured in regional GDP. Reveals US $5.1 billion of externalized value. Demonstrates governance risk from accounting invisibility.
geographic_focus: Western Indian Ocean (Northern Mozambique Channel region)

metadata.notes: Critical evidence of ecosystem value externality; demonstrates policy relevance of ecosystem accounting for WIO

sources.title: Western Indian Ocean Marine Protected Area (MPA) Outlook
sources.abstract: Regional assessment of progress toward Aichi Target 11 and SDG 14.5. Documents 143 MPAs covering 7% of combined EEZ. Identifies MPAs as primary focus for ecosystem condition accounts due to high biodiversity and service provision concentration.

metadata.notes: Key reference for PA-focused ecosystem condition accounting in WIO region

sources.title: Global Assessment of Environmental-Economic Accounting: 2023-2025 Progress Reports
sources.abstract: Annual global assessments tracking SEEA implementation progress. 2023: 90 countries implementing SEEA (41 with EA/thematic accounts). 2025: 98 countries, 71 with regular publication (Stage III). Shows 77% of non-implementing countries plan future adoption. Key metric showing global momentum toward ecosystem accounting.

metadata.notes: Critical reference for demonstrating global momentum and legitimacy of SEEA EA framework

sources.title: Coral Reef Fish Biomass Reference Levels: A Global Meta-Analysis
sources.abstract: Meta-analysis of coral reef fish biomass across global reefs, establishing reference levels for undisturbed and managed reefs. Frequently cited for fish biomass benchmarking. Reference level of 500 kg/ha used for Indo-Pacific.

metadata.notes: Critical reference for Madagascar coral reef fish condition baseline; 500 kg/ha commonly cited but may underestimate total reef fish

sources.title: Towards Ecosystem Accounts for Uganda
sources.publisher: Uganda Bureau of Statistics / World Bank
sources.abstract: Benchmarking report on the development of Experimental Ecosystem Accounts for Uganda, produced through the Uganda Natural Capital Accounting (NCA) program supported by the World Bank's WAVES Partnership (2018-2020). Describes results of the first iteration of experimental ecosystem accounts for forest and wetland ecosystems using the InVEST model. Demonstrates how ecosystem accounts can inform policy-making and ecosystem management through land cover accounting, conservation area assessments, and identification of forest type representation across river basins. Provides baseline data for tracking progress toward Vision 2040 and informing development strategies linking environmental, social and economic policies.
sources.methodology: InVEST modeling, land cover analysis, experimental ecosystem accounts

metadata.notes: Regional precedent for ecosystem accounting in East Africa; demonstrates SEEA EA implementation at national scale; directly applicable methodologies for Madagascar

sources.title: Accounting for ecosystem services and asset value: pilot accounts for KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa
sources.abstract: Peer-reviewed pilot study compiling monetary ecosystem accounts for KwaZulu-Natal Province, South Africa, to highlight data, methodological and process issues in ecosystem accounting compilation. Accounts cover 11 ecosystem services (wild biomass, reared animal production, cultivation/silviculture, nature-based tourism, property value, carbon storage/sequestration, pollination, flow regulation, sediment retention, water quality amelioration, flood attenuation) at 100×100m spatial resolution. Combined annual ecosystem service value estimated at R52.5 billion (12% of provincial GDP) in 2011. Study reveals methodological challenges including lack of subsistence production data, landcover/government statistics mismatches, ecosystem condition measurement reliability, and hydrological modeling scale issues. Demonstrates critical decline in ecosystem services over accounting period due to ecosystem condition changes.
sources.methodology: monetary ecosystem accounts, spatial accounting (100×100m units), InVEST and other ecosystem service models, valuation methods aligned with System of National Accounts

metadata.data_source: PDF extraction from Academic folder; One Ecosystem journal
metadata.notes: High-quality peer-reviewed subnational ecosystem accounting study; directly applicable to Madagascar provincial/watershed-scale accounts; demonstrates methodological challenges and solutions

sources.title: Towards ecosystem accounts for Rwanda: Tracking 25 years of change in flows and potential supply of ecosystem services
sources.abstract: Multi-author peer-reviewed study developing ecosystem service models to quantify ecosystem condition and physical supply components of ecosystem accounts in Rwanda (1990-2015). Applied InVEST carbon storage, sediment delivery, nutrient delivery, water yield models to map nationwide ES supply changes. Quantified flows of sediment, water, and nutrients to 96 hydroelectric dams, irrigation dams, and water treatment plants. Key findings: all ecosystem services declined over 25-year period, primarily driven by forest-to-cropland conversion. Declines most pronounced 1990-2000 and 2010-2015; ES relatively stable 2000-2010 except nutrient exports. Over 42% of Rwanda's water-use sites experienced upstream sediment/flow increases above national average (2010-2015). Results provide national ES trends baseline, quantify implications for water-dependent industries, and demonstrate importance of protected areas in safeguarding ES flows.
sources.methodology: InVEST ecosystem service models, land cover analysis, 25-year temporal trend analysis, hydroelectric/water treatment impact assessment

metadata.data_source: PDF extraction from Academic folder; People and Nature journal
metadata.pdf_path: Academic/People and Nature - 2019 - Bagstad - Towards ecosystem accounts for Rwanda  Tracking 25 years of change in flows and.pdf
metadata.notes: Rigorous peer-reviewed study demonstrating 25-year ecosystem accounting for East African country; shows land use change impacts on ecosystem services; highly relevant to Madagascar temporal trend analysis

evidence_clusters.claim_statement: SEEA EA represents a transformative international standard for ecosystem accounting adopted globally with increasing implementation momentum through 2025.
evidence_clusters.evidence_strength: Unanimous institutional adoption; tracked by UNSD; 98 countries implementing by 2025

evidence_clusters.claim_statement: Nairobi Convention member states demonstrate varied but advancing adoption of SEEA EA, with South Africa as mature paradigm, Kenya and Mauritius in active implementation, and Madagascar/Mozambique developing foundational accounts.
evidence_clusters.evidence_strength: Multiple country-level case studies; phased implementation documented; clear progression visible

evidence_clusters.claim_statement: Madagascar has foundational SEEA capacity through WAVES-supported forest and water accounts, with recent ecosystem accounting studies (Mahavavy-Kinkony) demonstrating technical sophistication and readiness for expansion to marine ecosystems.
evidence_clusters.evidence_strength: WAVES accounts are established; Mahavavy-Kinkony study is recent and rigorous; marine extension is logical next step

evidence_clusters.claim_statement: WIO marine and coastal ecosystems deliver substantial economic value (NoCaMo: $5.7B annually) that is largely externalized in national GDP, demonstrating critical policy relevance of ecosystem accounting.
evidence_clusters.evidence_strength: Regional assessment with credible research partners; demonstrates governance relevance; comparable to global findings

evidence_clusters.claim_statement: Coral reef fish biomass reference levels in the Indo-Pacific, commonly cited as 500 kg/ha, may underestimate total reef fish production in some regions due to meta-analysis methodology limitations.
evidence_clusters.evidence_strength: Single peer-reviewed source; widely used but requires Madagascar-specific validation
gaps: Limited data on historical pristine reefs
Climate change impacts on baseline uncertain

integration_notes.with_policy_rag: Sources should be cross-linked with policy RAG to show which academic evidence supports Madagascar policy commitments (Nairobi Convention, NBSAP, Blue Policy, etc.)
integration_notes.publication_workflow: Use these sources as foundation for 'Environmental-Economic Accounting Transitions' policy brief and any peer-reviewed publications on Madagascar coral reef accounts
next_steps: Verify accessibility of all paywalled sources
Add grey literature (reports, theses) from WAVES, UNSD databases
Extract specific quotes and page numbers for each claim in draft publication
Create evidence clusters for each major claim in policy brief
Identify gaps: Madagascar-specific fish biomass data, island economy SEEA EA implementations
